TextENGNew: ……Spring.
Like the swept stone steps drawing breath, the freshly fallen cherry blossoms color them softly.
There was a loveliness to it beyond mere beauty, and the warmth it stirred in me as I climbed was something small but true.
I looked up, and found the cherry blossoms in full bloom.
The haze pattern, like a dreaming weeping willow, was beautiful—and the faint sweet fragrance was wonderfully refreshing.
Caught in the familiar scenery and the breath of the east wind, I walked slowly about the shrine grounds for a while, savoring the clear air.
changeReason: New translation adds 'the breath of the east wind' from the Japanese 東風 (kochi/east wind), a significant poetic detail the original omits.

TextENGNew: I turned, and there at the far edge of the grounds stood the shrine hall.
Ancient enough to carry the weight of ages, it must have been lovingly maintained by the villagers. Even in its weathering, it stood with a composed, settled dignity.
――Ten years had passed since that day.
When dawn broke on that night of nightmares, the villagers armed themselves, gathered in force, and went to Onigafuchi Swamp.
There they found a vast number of bandits' corpses… and amid them, fallen as though he had died fighting them, the body of Furude Riku. And the tattered, shredded remnants of a miko's robe.
"C-could this be…?!"
Confronted with the scene of that tragedy, they recalled how only days before they had suspected the wife of the Furude shrine's priest of being a demon… and they were struck dumb with horror.
By their own suspicions, they had driven the couple to…
And the two of them had gone to battle a band of wandering bandits posing as demons—to prove their innocence—and lost their lives…
"Ri—Riku…, Hanyuu…?! Why—…why, why, WHY—!! Ahhh—!!"
Shino, who had rushed to the scene upon hearing the news, clung to the bodies and wept until her voice and tears were utterly spent.
changeReason: New captures the Japanese 涙と声がかれるほどに (until voice and tears were exhausted), adding specificity that 'broke down sobbing' lacks.

TextENGNew: …Afterward, the whole village turned out to search for Riku's wife.
But her blood trail and footprints came to an end… at the edge of a cliff overlooking the river.
She had likely fallen from there along with the bandits—that was the villagers' conclusion.
Upon learning this, Shino fell into an even deeper despair, and wailed as those who knew her strength of spirit had never seen her wail before…
"I'm sorry, I'm so sorry, Riku, Hanyuu…! If only I had spoken up more firmly to everyone on your behalf…! If only I had told you more clearly that I believed in you…!"
changeReason: New translation changes 'made a better case to win the others over for you' to 'told you more clearly that I believed in you', matching the Japanese あなたたちのことを信じていると、もっと言ってあげていれば (if only I'd told you more that I believed in you).

TextENGNew: "I'm sorry, I'm so sorry…!!"
"What did we do to those two…"
changeReason: New adds 'to those two' matching the Japanese あの二人に (to those two), specifying the object of their guilt.

TextENGNew: "…We did them a terrible wrong. It's our fault…"
Seeing this, they each understood—their needless suspicions had been all but the death of that couple—and they repented of their failings with deep remorse.
Perhaps as an act of atonement for that sin… the orphaned daughter left behind by the Furude family—Ouka—was taken under the village's protection as the daughter of those who had saved them.

With Shino at the head, she was entrusted to the guardianship of the Kimiyoshi family and the Sonozaki village officials, and was raised with great care so that one day she might manage the Furude Shrine entirely on her own.
All of that… I could only watch over from afar.
…After the battle with the demons, I had lost nearly all the power that sustained my physical form.

``It was not quite the state of a 'disembodied soul,' but it became difficult to maintain the 'substance' needed to be visible even to the people of this land—and so, naturally, I came to be treated as one who had vanished.''
changeReason: New translates 遊離体 as 'disembodied soul' while original uses 'free body'; also new adds explanation about 'substance' (実体) needed to be visible, providing clearer context.

TextENGNew: There may have been a spiritual dimension to it as well. It was likely I would never fully recover, and if I pushed myself too hard, there was the risk my body would run out of control.
changeReason: Original implies temporary incomplete recovery; new says 'likely I would never fully recover', matching the Japanese おそらく完全に回復することはなく (probably would never fully recover).

TextENGNew: And so, wishing for Ouka's happiness, I crossed to the mainland, and while receiving treatment through the remaining Lynos, I continued to fight the mixed-bloods called demons—the Grifys—or those who falsely bore that name.
changeReason: New adds 'mixed-bloods' (混血/Grifys) as a category label and distinguishes between the Grifys themselves and 'those who falsely bore that name', matching the Japanese それを騙る連中.

TextENGNew: How much blood I have shed through these years… I choose not to speak of it. Even so, as long as those who would do evil persist, tragedies like Riku's will come to pass—great or small.
changeReason: New adds 'great or small' matching the Japanese 大小の類を問わず (regardless of scale), and uses more certain 'will' vs original's 'could'.

TextENGNew: With that conviction, I took up my sword and used what power and time remained to me to travel the lands and cut out the seeds of calamity wherever they lay…
(…So I, too, am atoning for my sins.)
That thought brought an involuntary smile to my lips.
And then, turning my gaze without thinking, I caught sight of the cherry tree Riku and I had once looked up at together, and I quietly drew near and gently laid my hand against its trunk.
"…The cherry blossoms are blooming, Riku. Even without you here, this year, again——"
Old memories began to stir. The poem by Michizane that the two of us had spoken of together.
……You know, Riku.
If there is truly a place called heaven.
Then these cherry trees surely go on blooming every year to carry word to you, there among those blossoms—word that all is well.
changeReason: Original says blossoms tell Hanyuu that Riku is 'doing well'; new says blossoms carry word TO Riku that 'all is well'. The Japanese says the trees bloom to convey 元気だよ (I'm well) TO him in heaven, so new is correct.

TextENGNew: Just as I go on living, carrying within me my feelings for you, and my memories——
"……. Riku… would you mind, just a little, if I cried…?"
…Strange.
Not so long ago, merely thinking of Riku was enough to bring tears, unstoppable.
But now I could no longer cry unless I deliberately called up the memory of the happiest times and let myself sink into the grief of them.
Was it that I had grown stronger?
Or only that I had grown numb?
Even I… was not quite sure.
Even so, my feelings for Riku had not faded—even now they lived, steadily, within my chest.
The days spent with him felt so close it might have been yesterday, and yet time had grown so distant I could scarcely feel it.
"Riku……"
Would it have been better, I wonder, never to have met him at all?
If I had never loved him, never been joined with him—would I have been spared this grief?
…Not once have I thought that.
I was truly glad to have met Riku.
It was none other than him who showed me the path to living as myself, as who I truly am…
As I murmured these things to myself—
A single pink petal floated down and settled softly on my shoulder. The same sweet scent as that day. Without thinking, a smile broke across my face.
Rustle……
Just then.
I sensed the faintest footsteps behind me, and quietly turned around—
And at the same moment, smiled despite myself.
What was I thinking. My form should no longer be visible to anyone…
And yet.
"…Who are you?"
The little girl standing there as I turned had her round, bright eyes wide open—
And with a small tilt of her head and an innocent smile on her face… she was looking at me.
That face.
That gaze.
…I remembered it well.
How could I ever forget…
"You can see me…? Me…?"
"Yes, I can! Is this your father's tree?"
changeReason: Original has Ouka asking 'Do you have some business at my father's tree?'; new changes to 'Is this your father's tree?' — different question with different implication about whether Ouka already knows it's her father's tree.

TextENGNew: At that.
Gazing intently at the little girl's bright, smiling face, struggling to hold back the feelings welling up inside me, I steadied my breath quietly—and returned her smile.
For a moment she blinked in surprise—then her face broke open into the most radiant smile, and with a bright 'Nipah~☆' she gave a cheerful little hop.
changeReason: New includes the iconic 'Nipah~☆' exclamation from the Japanese にぱ～☆ that the original omitted entirely.

TextENGNew: "——How do you do. I am the guardian deity of this shrine—the one you call Oyashiro-sama. And you are?"
"I'm Ouka! Furude Ouka. Nipah~♪"
…The time that had stood still within me began, slowly, to move once more.
